zh-tw.title: 工程師笑話大全
zh-tw.description: 給工程師和懂工程師的人笑的，每一則都是真實人生的血淚結晶

en.title: Programmer Jokes
en.description: For developers, by developers — jokes only make sense if you've stared at a screen too long

items.sourceNote: Classic programmer logic joke — apple/orange variant widely shared

zh-tw.content:
主管叫工程師去買五個便當，說：「如果有賣素食的話，買三個就好。」
工程師回來手上拿著三個便當。
主管：「為什麼只買三個？！」
工程師：「因為有賣素食的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給公司同事或工程師朋友，十個有九個秒懂還會說「這很合理」。
variations: 也可以換成：『如果有折扣就多買一份』，工程師就多買了一份折扣券回來

en.content:
A manager told the developer: "Go grab five coffees. If they have cold brew, just get three."
The developer came back with three coffees.
Manager: "Why only three?!"
Developer: "They had cold brew."
en.editorNote: Send this to any developer and watch them nod and say 'that's correct logic.'
variations: Try it with: 'get five if they have oat milk, otherwise get two' — same result, different milk.

items.sourceNote: PTT joke board — programmer lies //TODO classic

zh-tw.content:
程式設計師有三大謊言：
第一：「這只是暫時的解法，之後會改掉的。」
第二：「這段很簡單，很快就寫好了。」
第三：「我在程式碼裡面寫了說明。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給剛踏入業界的工程師新人，讓他提早做好心理準備。
variations: 還有第四條：『我測試過了，沒問題』

en.content:
The three biggest lies in programming:
1. "This is just a quick temporary fix."
2. "It'll only take five minutes."
3. "I left comments explaining everything."
en.editorNote: Send to a junior developer on day one. Consider it orientation.
variations: Add a fourth: 'I tested it thoroughly and it's fine.'

items.sourceNote: PTT — 拜雍正治八阿哥 (bug = 八哥) joke

zh-tw.content:
有人問工程師：「除錯的時候要拜什麼神比較靈？」
工程師不假思索地說：「拜雍正皇帝。」
「為什麼？」
「因為他最在行對付八阿哥（Bug）。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 歷史梗加IT梗的雙層笑點，傳給有點歷史知識的工程師朋友特別有效果。
variations: 也可以說拜康熙，因為他是Bug的爸爸（八阿哥的爹）

en.content:
Someone asked a developer: "What do you pray to before a debugging session?"
The developer didn't hesitate: "Stack Overflow. Patron saint of the stuck, the lost, and the mysteriously broken."
en.editorNote: Works best delivered completely deadpan. Any developer who's rage-searched at 2 AM will feel this instantly.
variations: Follow up: 'And if Stack Overflow is also down? Then the bug has already won.'

items.sourceNote: INSIDE — 59 programming quotes, classic 'if it works don't touch it'

zh-tw.content:
工程師的最高信條：
「它現在跑得起來。不要問為什麼。不要碰它。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 每個工程師心中都有那段不知道為什麼能動的程式碼，這句話是共鳴神器。
variations: 進階版：『我已經三個月沒動那段了，系統還是好好的，我打算帶著這個秘密進棺材。』

en.content:
The developer's golden commandment:
"It's running. Don't ask why. Don't touch it."
en.editorNote: Every developer has code they're afraid to look at. This is their anthem.
variations: Extended version: 'I haven't touched that module in six months and everything still works. I'm taking that secret to my grave.'

items.sourceNote: TechOrange — 15 programmer jokes, bugs breeding joke variant

zh-tw.content:
工程師除錯的人生：
修好一個Bug，冒出兩個。
修好兩個，跑出四個。
工程師對此有一個專業術語：「正常。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在debug的朋友，讓他知道他並不孤單，這是行業標準。
variations: 還有一種說法：『我不是在增加功能，我是在繁殖Bug的天敵。』

en.content:
The debugging lifecycle:
Fix one bug, two appear.
Fix two, four show up.
Developers have a technical term for this: "normal."
en.editorNote: Send to a friend who's been debugging all day. Solidarity.
variations: Also known as: 'I'm not creating bugs. I'm discovering ones that were always there.'

items.sourceNote: TechOrange — Stack Overflow dependency joke

zh-tw.content:
工程師的除錯四步驟：
第一步：Google。
第二步：找到Stack Overflow的答案。
第三步：貼上來試試看。
第四步：祈禱。

如果不行，重複第一步。
zh-tw.editorNote: 這個流程不需要解釋，每個工程師都有同樣的制約反應。
variations: 有時候第四步會改成：重新開機然後假裝沒事

en.content:
Developer debugging protocol:
Step 1: Google the error.
Step 2: Find a Stack Overflow answer from 2012.
Step 3: Paste it in.
Step 4: Hope.

Repeat until it works.
en.editorNote: This is not a joke. This is documentation.
variations: Alternate step 4: restart the computer and pretend it never happened.

items.sourceNote: INSIDE — code comments joke, God and the programmer

zh-tw.content:
資深工程師看著一段六個月前自己寫的程式碼：
「我當初寫這個的時候，只有我和上帝知道這在做什麼。」
同事：「那現在呢？」
工程師：「現在只剩上帝知道了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 自嘲用，技術分享會或Onboarding場合特別好笑。
variations: 反過來也成立：『新來的工程師看我的程式碼，跟我說他不懂。我也不懂，我是最近才接手的。』

en.content:
A senior developer opened code they wrote eight months ago:
"When I wrote this, only God and I knew what it did."
Colleague: "And now?"
Developer: "Now only God knows."
en.editorNote: Great for tech talks or any onboarding session. Humbling and relatable.
variations: Alternate ending: 'And God is being very quiet about it.'

items.sourceNote: ProgrammerHumor.io — git commit messages joke widely shared

zh-tw.content:
某工程師的Git提交記錄（由上到下）：
「修正」
「再修一次」
「這次真的修好了」
「為什麼又壞了」
「終極修正版」
「最終版本_真的最終」
「不要問我發生了什麼事」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給用Git的朋友，或是在工作群組裡找到共鳴，笑著哭出來。
variations: 也有人的提交記錄是：「WIP」、「WIP2」、「WIP_final」、「please work」、「ok it works bye」

en.content:
A developer's recent Git commit history:
"fix"
"fix again"
"actually fixed this time"
"WHY IS IT BROKEN AGAIN"
"final fix"
"final_fix_v2"
"do not look at this commit"
en.editorNote: Share in any developer group chat. The reactions will be silent nods of recognition.
variations: Some people just go: 'WIP', 'WIP2', 'please work', 'ok fine', 'it's someone else's problem now'

items.sourceNote: PTT — programmer diet and caffeine dependence jokes

zh-tw.content:
工程師的飲食金字塔：
最底層：咖啡（算液體，補充水分）
中層：泡麵（碳水化合物，能量來源）
頂層：能量飲料（算維生素，因為有B群）
偶爾奢侈一下：叫外送然後在螢幕前吃。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合轉貼在同事群組，不健康但有共鳴。
variations: 有人說工程師其實還有第四個食物：截圖存下來的美食圖（根本沒時間出去吃）

en.content:
The developer food pyramid:
Base: Coffee (hydration)
Middle: Instant noodles (carbs for energy)
Top: Energy drinks (vitamins, probably)
Occasional treat: Delivery food eaten at the desk.

The desk is the dining table.
en.editorNote: Share with your team. Unhealthy but universally relatable.
variations: Some developers add a fourth tier: food they photographed but never had time to actually eat.

items.sourceNote: PTT — programmers working overtime and weekends joke

zh-tw.content:
老闆：「優秀的工程師不需要假日，因為他們熱愛工作。」
工程師：「對，所以我假日也在修你上線前要求緊急加的功能。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 職場共鳴神器，但不要直接傳給老闆。
variations: 延伸版：『工程師連睡夢中都在想程式碼，這叫熱情。PM連夢裡都要傳訊息，這叫擾民。』

en.content:
Manager: "Great engineers don't need days off. They love what they do."
Developer: "Right. Which is why I'm fixing the feature you requested at 11 PM Friday — on Saturday."
en.editorNote: Share with coworkers who get it. Not recommended for managers.
variations: Follow up: 'Developers dream in code. That's passion. PMs text during dreams. That's harassment.'

items.sourceNote: CSS is unpredictable — classic dev community meme

zh-tw.content:
CSS就是這樣的東西：
你只是想讓一個按鈕靠右一點點，
結果整個頁面的版面都跑掉了，
導覽列不見了，
背景圖片跑去角落哭泣，
而那個按鈕還是在原本的位置。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給前端工程師，他一定會說「這不是笑話，這是我的日常」。
variations: 有時候解法是：先把所有CSS砍掉，再一行一行加回去，直到找到那個害你崩潰的地方。

en.content:
CSS: you try to move a button two pixels to the right.
The footer disappears.
The nav bar rotates.
The background image goes to mourn in the corner.
The button is exactly where it was.
en.editorNote: Send to any front-end developer. They will not laugh. They will cry.
variations: The fix is always: delete everything, add it back line by line, and find the one rule that was haunting you.

items.sourceNote: Programmer sleep joke — counting bugs not sheep

zh-tw.content:
「你昨晚睡了幾個小時？」
工程師抬起頭：「我不數小時。」
「那你數什麼？」
「我數Bug。昨晚睡前數了四十七個。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 深夜加班時傳給正在debug的同事，療癒系自嘲。
variations: 有工程師說：「我數羊也沒用，因為我一直想著那隻羊為什麼不在陣列的正確索引位置上。」

en.content:
"How many hours did you sleep last night?"
The developer looked up: "I don't count hours."
"What do you count?"
"Bugs. I fell asleep at forty-three."
en.editorNote: Send late at night to a developer who's still online. They'll feel seen.
variations: Some developers count sheep but keep wondering why the sheep aren't sorted correctly.

items.sourceNote: INSIDE — documentation state joke

zh-tw.content:
軟體文件只有兩種狀態：
一種是早就過期的，
另一種是根本還沒寫的。

而寫文件的工程師，
通常已經離職了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 在工程師聚會上說，現場所有人都會沉默一秒，然後一起笑出來。
variations: 補充：第三種狀態是『文件裡面的連結全部都404了』

en.content:
Software documentation exists in two states:
Outdated, or nonexistent.

The engineer who wrote it?
Already left the company.
en.editorNote: Say this at any developer gathering. There will be a moment of silence, then recognition.
variations: Third state: the documentation exists but every link in it is 404.

items.sourceNote: ProgrammerHumor.io — it works but I have no idea why

zh-tw.content:
工程師修完Bug，緊張地說：「好，我覺得這樣應該可以了。」
同事：「你知道為什麼修好了嗎？」
工程師停頓了一秒：「……不知道。但它現在能跑了，我決定不繼續追究。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 每個工程師都有這種「別問，能動就好」的時刻，分享給懂的人。
variations: 進階版：『這個Bug我改了一行空白，就消失了。我沒有刪掉那行，因為我怕它回來。』

en.content:
Developer after an hour of debugging: "Okay, I think this is fixed."
Colleague: "Do you know why it's fixed?"
Developer, after a long pause: "No. But it's working, and I've decided not to dig further."
en.editorNote: Every developer has this moment. Share it with someone who's lived it.
variations: Alternate version: 'I added a blank line and the bug disappeared. I'm not removing it. I don't want it to come back.'

items.sourceNote: PTT — programmer blog writing as replacement for social sharing

zh-tw.content:
有人問：「為什麼那麼多工程師喜歡寫技術部落格？」
老工程師嘆了口氣說：
「因為解完一個技術難題之後，
沒有人可以告訴。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 溫柔的自嘲，適合轉給單身或社恐的工程師朋友，或是在技術社群中分享。
variations: 有人說：『也有工程師是為了讓未來的自己可以找到解法。工程師的最大敵人是半年前的自己。』

en.content:
Someone asked: "Why do so many developers write tech blogs?"
A senior developer sighed:
"Because after solving a really hard problem,
there's no one to tell."
en.editorNote: A gentle, relatable joke about developer social life. Share with developer friends or in a dev community.
variations: Alternative theory: 'They write blogs so their future self can find the solution. A developer's biggest enemy is their six-months-ago self.'

items.sourceNote: AI-assisted development humor — widely shared format in 2025-2026

zh-tw.content:
工程師：「以後AI幫我寫程式，我可以輕鬆很多了！」

三個月後，同一個工程師：
「以後AI幫我解釋AI寫的程式，我可以輕鬆很多了！」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給用過AI寫程式的工程師朋友，他們會笑出眼淚。
variations: 延伸版：『下一步：讓AI去解釋AI對AI寫的程式碼的解釋，工程師負責喝咖啡。』

en.content:
Developer: "AI is going to write my code for me. Things are going to get so much easier!"

Same developer, three months later:
"AI is going to explain the code that AI wrote for me. Things are going to get so much easier!"
en.editorNote: Share with any developer who uses AI tools. They will laugh with pain in their eyes.
variations: Next level: 'I'll have AI explain AI's explanation of AI's code. I'll just supervise the coffee.'

items.sourceNote: Meeting culture vs. developer productivity — universal tech workplace joke

zh-tw.content:
工程師參加了三小時的線上會議後，打開電腦，翻開任務清單。
上面什麼都沒有改變。
他在備忘錄裡補上一行：「這場會議可以改成一封Email。」
然後把這行刪掉。
因為他知道：反正沒有人會改的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 在工程師聚會上說，配上一臉淡然的表情，現場一定安靜兩秒再爆笑。
variations: 有工程師說：『我判斷一個會議值不值得開，標準就是——它的結論能不能用一句話概括。如果可以，那它本來就應該是一封Email。』

en.content:
After a three-hour video call, the developer opened their laptop and checked the task list.
Nothing had changed.
They added a note: "This meeting could have been an email."
Then deleted it.
Because they knew: no one was going to change anything anyway.
en.editorNote: Deliver this with a flat, resigned expression. The room will go quiet for two seconds before erupting.
variations: One developer's rule: 'If the outcome can be summarized in one sentence, it should have been an email.' They have never attended fewer meetings.

items.sourceNote: Dark mode / light attracts bugs — popular developer one-liner

zh-tw.content:
有人問工程師：「你為什麼這麼執著用深色模式？」
工程師停頓了一秒，認真地說：
「因為光會吸引蟲（Bug）。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 講的時候要一臉嚴肅，這樣後勁才夠強。
variations: 補充理由：『而且深色模式讓人看起來更專業，Bug也不好意思來。』

en.content:
Someone asked a developer: "Why are you so committed to dark mode?"
The developer paused, then said with complete seriousness:
"Because light attracts bugs."
en.editorNote: Deadpan delivery is essential. The joke lands harder with a straight face.
variations: Additional reason: 'Dark mode also makes you look more serious. Bugs feel unwelcome.'

items.sourceNote: Variable naming as one of the hardest problems in CS — widely cited

zh-tw.content:
工程師寫程式最花時間的三件事：
第一：找Bug。
第二：修Bug。
第三：想變數名稱。

前兩件事有工具可以幫忙。
第三件事是靈魂的拷問。
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼在工程師群組裡，秒引發集體共鳴。
variations: 計算機科學界有句名言：『電腦科學只有兩件難事：快取失效、替東西命名，還有多退一的錯誤。』

en.content:
The three biggest time sinks in software development:
1. Finding the bug.
2. Fixing the bug.
3. Naming the variable.

The first two have tools.
The third one is a crisis of the soul.
en.editorNote: Drop this in any developer group chat. Instant recognition.
variations: There's a famous saying in CS: 'There are only two hard problems: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors.'

items.sourceNote: Deployment anxiety — universal developer ritual before going live

zh-tw.content:
工程師部署前的完整心理流程：
「確認備份了嗎？確認了。」
「測試過了嗎？測試過了。」
「那我按下去了喔。」
「……
好，沒事。」
「……那為什麼我還是這麼緊張？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛部署完的工程師，他會直接崩潰大笑
variations: 延伸版：按下去之後的五分鐘，是工程師人生中最長的五分鐘。不管有沒有出事都是。

en.content:
The full mental checklist before deployment:
'Did I back everything up? Yes.'
'Did I test it? Yes.'
'Okay, I'm pressing it.'
'...'
'Everything's fine.'
'...So why am I still shaking?'
en.editorNote: Send this to a developer right after they deploy. Expect immediate laughter or tears.
variations: The five minutes after pressing deploy are the longest five minutes in a developer's life — regardless of what happens.

items.sourceNote: Learning new frameworks cycle — classic developer experience

zh-tw.content:
工程師學新框架的固定流程：
第一步：聽說這個框架很棒。
第二步：花三天設定環境。
第三步：成功跑出Hello World，覺得自己很厲害。
第四步：嘗試做真正的功能。
第五步：發現官方文件跟實際行為完全不一樣。
第六步：聽說有一個更新更好的框架。
第一步：聽說那個框架很棒。
zh-tw.editorNote: 工程師社群分享神器，每一行都是真實人生
variations: 補充第二點五步：環境設定失敗，重新安裝Node，順便更新一下版本，然後舊的東西全部壞掉。

en.content:
The developer's framework learning cycle:
Step 1: Hear that this framework is amazing.
Step 2: Spend three days on environment setup.
Step 3: Run Hello World. Feel unstoppable.
Step 4: Try to build something real.
Step 5: Discover the docs don't match the actual behavior.
Step 6: Hear about a newer, better framework.
Step 1: Hear that this framework is amazing.
en.editorNote: Post in any developer community. Every single line will resonate.
variations: Add step 2.5: environment setup fails, you reinstall Node, update the version, and now everything that used to work doesn't.

items.sourceNote: 'Works on my machine' — one of the most notorious phrases in software development

zh-tw.content:
「為什麼測試環境沒問題，上到正式環境就壞了？」
工程師A：「我在我電腦上是好的啊。」
工程師B：「那把你的電腦部署上去就好了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: IT部門的永恆話題，傳給有過這種經歷的同事，秒懂秒崩潰
variations: 後來有人真的做了一個貼紙，寫著『Works on my machine』，然後要認證自己電腦是正式伺服器。

en.content:
"Why does it work fine in testing but break in production?"
Developer A: "It works on my machine."
Developer B: "Then let's just ship your machine."
en.editorNote: An eternal software joke. Every developer has lived this exact conversation.
variations: Someone actually made a 'Works on My Machine' certification sticker. It is both a joke and a cry for help.

items.sourceNote: Developer work-life balance and the myth of 'quick tasks'

zh-tw.content:
PM說：「這個改動很簡單，只是換個顏色。」
工程師打開程式碼。
這個顏色散落在四十七個檔案裡。
三個是寫死的。
兩個是從外部API抓來的。
一個根本不知道是從哪裡來的。
工程師：「好，我評估一下，大概要三天。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給PM或不懂技術的主管，讓他們稍微理解一下「簡單改動」的真實面貌
variations: 同場加映：『只是加一個按鈕』，工程師聽到這句話的瞬間，眼神死掉了。

en.content:
PM: "This is a simple change — just update the color."
The developer opens the codebase.
The color is in forty-seven files.
Three are hardcoded.
Two are pulled from an external API.
One has no traceable origin.
Developer: "Okay, I'd estimate about three days."
en.editorNote: Share with PMs or non-technical managers — a window into what 'small changes' actually look like
variations: Same energy: 'Just add a button.' The moment a developer hears this, something behind their eyes goes quiet.

items.sourceNote: Password complexity requirements — universal IT security frustration

zh-tw.content:
IT部門說新密碼必須包含：大寫字母、小寫字母、數字、特殊符號，且不得少於十二位。
工程師盯著螢幕想了三分鐘，輸入了一個自認為很聰明的密碼。
三個月後，他完全不記得了。

現在他的密碼是：「忘記了再設@1」
zh-tw.editorNote: 在IT安全培訓後分享，現場每個人都會沉默一秒，然後一起點頭
variations: 密碼強度：超強。記住密碼的能力：超弱。這就是人生。

en.content:
The new IT policy: passwords must contain uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters, and be at least twelve characters long.
The developer stared at the screen for three minutes and typed something clever.
Three months later, they had absolutely no idea what it was.

Current password: 'ForgotAgain@1'
en.editorNote: Share right after any IT security training. Every person in the room will nod quietly.
variations: Password strength: very strong. Ability to remember password: very weak. Such is life.

items.sourceNote: Code review culture — 'minor change' that isn't minor

zh-tw.content:
工程師提交Code Review，備註：「小改動，幫我確認一下。」
審查者打開來：
四個檔案被修改。
三個新函式被加進來。
一段沒有人知道是做什麼的程式碼被刪掉了。
一半的變數被重新命名。

審查者盯著螢幕三十秒，回覆：「LGTM 👍」
zh-tw.editorNote: Code Review文化的完美縮影，傳給有過這種經歷的工程師，對方秒懂
variations: 什麼叫做『小改動』？就是改了很多但你不想解釋的那種。

en.content:
Developer submits a pull request with the description: "Minor change, quick review please."
The reviewer opens it:
Four files modified.
Three new functions added.
A mysterious chunk of code deleted with no explanation.
Half the variables renamed.

The reviewer stares at the screen for thirty seconds, then comments: "LGTM 👍"
en.editorNote: The truest code review experience. Send to any developer who's been on either side of this.
variations: What is a 'minor change'? Something you changed a lot but don't want to explain.

items.sourceNote: Developer sleep schedule — binary hour joke

zh-tw.content:
有人問工程師：「你平常幾點睡？」
工程師說：「00:00到01:00之間。」
「什麼，差一個小時？這範圍也太大了吧？」
「是啊。視今天的Bug數量而定。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 深夜還在線上的工程師最懂這種感覺，傳給他讓他知道不孤單
variations: 有時候00:00多睡一點，有時候01:00還在跟bug搏鬥。工程師的時間是動態的。

en.content:
Someone asked a developer: "What time do you usually sleep?"
Developer: "Somewhere between midnight and 1 AM."
"That's an hour window — why so vague?"
"Depends on how many bugs I had today."
en.editorNote: Send to a developer who's still online at midnight. They will feel deeply understood.
variations: Sometimes closer to midnight, sometimes still fighting a bug at 1 AM. A developer's schedule is dynamic.

items.sourceNote: Recursion explanation joke — classic CS humor

zh-tw.content:
有人問工程師：「你怎麼跟非工程師解釋什麼是遞迴？」
工程師：「要解釋遞迴，你必須先理解遞迴。」
「……然後呢？」
「然後要理解遞迴，你必須先理解遞迴。」
「……我懂了。」
「對，你剛剛體驗了遞迴。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 電腦科學課的保留曲目，在解釋技術概念的場合說效果絕佳，讓聽的人真的懂了遞迴是什麼
variations: 遞迴的最大危險：忘記設定終止條件，然後你就會一直解釋下去，直到對方的耐心用完。

en.content:
Someone asked a developer: "How do you explain recursion to a non-developer?"
Developer: "To explain recursion, you first need to understand recursion."
"...And then?"
"Then to understand recursion, you need to explain recursion."
"...I think I get it."
"Good. You just experienced recursion."
en.editorNote: A CS classroom classic — use when explaining technical concepts; your audience will actually learn what recursion is
variations: The greatest risk of recursion: forgetting the base case. Then you'll keep explaining forever until the other person gives up.

items.sourceNote: AI coding assistant humor — widely discussed in developer circles 2025-2026

zh-tw.content:
自從用了 AI 輔助寫程式，工程師的工作效率提升了三倍。

讀 AI 生成的程式碼、搞清楚它在幹嘛、再重構一遍的時間，也提升了三倍。

淨成長：零。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在用 Copilot / Cursor 的工程師朋友，對方會先笑，再說「幹你說得對」
variations: AI 說：我幫你省時間。工程師說：謝謝，我現在花兩倍時間審查你的輸出。AI：那是你的問題。

en.content:
Since adopting AI-assisted coding, developer productivity tripled.

Time spent reading AI-generated code, figuring out what it does, and refactoring it also tripled.

Net gain: zero.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone using Copilot or Cursor — they'll laugh, then say 'honestly accurate'
variations: AI: 'I'll save you time.' Developer: 'Thanks, I now spend twice as long reviewing your output.' AI: 'That's a you problem.'

items.sourceNote: Classic developer estimation humor

zh-tw.content:
PM：這個功能需要多久？
工程師：三天。
PM：能不能兩天？
工程師：可以。

——兩週後功能完成——

PM：你說兩天啊？
工程師：你說可以壓縮，我說可以接受這個需求。
但沒有人說時間真的會縮短。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給 PM 或主管，讓他們感受一下工程師思維的精準性
variations: 進階版：『好的，沒問題』是工程師說過最有歧義的一句話——它從來不代表時間、資源或方法已經確認

en.content:
PM: How long for this feature?
Developer: Three days.
PM: Can you do it in two?
Developer: Sure.

— Two weeks later, feature ships —

PM: You said two days?
Developer: You asked if I could accept the timeline. I said yes. Nobody said the work would actually shrink.
en.editorNote: Share with a PM or manager — a window into how developers process verbal agreements
variations: Advanced insight: 'Sure, no problem' is the most ambiguous sentence in software development. It confirms nothing.

items.sourceNote: Remote work culture humor among developers

zh-tw.content:
遠端工作的工程師每天有三種狀態：

第一種：認真工作
第二種：假裝認真工作
第三種：Slack 和 YouTube 同時開著，在兩個視窗之間切換，維持最低限度的線上存在感

有研究顯示，第三種佔總工時的相當大比例。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給遠端工作的朋友，他看完不會回你，因為他剛好在切視窗
variations: 在家工作最誠實的設定：會議時打開鏡頭說「我在」，鏡頭關掉後才是真正的生活

en.content:
Remote developers exist in three modes:

Mode 1: Actually working
Mode 2: Looking like they're working
Mode 3: Slack and YouTube simultaneously open, toggling between them while maintaining minimal online presence

Research suggests Mode 3 accounts for a non-trivial portion of total working hours.
en.editorNote: Send to a remote-working friend — they won't reply immediately, because they're switching tabs
variations: Most honest remote setup: camera on during meetings to prove existence, camera off to resume actual life.

items.sourceNote: Classic bug-finding humor rewritten

zh-tw.content:
工程師追了三天的bug，終於修好了。
主管：「問題出在哪？」
工程師：「一個多餘的空格。」
主管：「……找到花了多久？」
工程師：「五分鐘。」
主管：「那三天在幹嘛？」
工程師：「接受它就是一個空格，花了兩天又二十三小時。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 工程師看了會沉默半秒然後點頭——這是真實體驗，不是誇張
variations: 補充：最崩潰的bug，往往不是最難找的，是找到了之後覺得「怎麼會是這個」的那種

en.content:
An engineer spent three days tracking down a bug. Finally fixed.
Manager: "What was the problem?"
Engineer: "An extra space."
Manager: "...How long to find it?"
Engineer: "Five minutes."
Manager: "Then what took three days?"
Engineer: "Accepting that it was a space. That took two days, twenty-three hours."
en.editorNote: Every developer will nod and go quiet for a moment — this is lived experience, not exaggeration
variations: The most devastating bugs aren't the hardest to find. They're the ones where, once found, you think: 'How was it this.'

items.sourceNote: Deadline and bug multiplication humor rewritten

zh-tw.content:
下午六點，工程師說：「我今天可以早點走，只剩最後一個小bug了。」

隔天早上九點，工程師還在辦公室。
同事：「那個bug修好了嗎？」
工程師：「修好了。」
同事：「那你怎麼還在？」
工程師：「修完之後出現三個新的。它們是那個bug的家人。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在修bug的同事，他看完會無聲地嘆一口氣然後繼續
variations: bug是社群動物：消滅一個，其餘的會聚集悼念，順便製造更多混亂

en.content:
At 6pm, an engineer said: "I can leave early today. Just one small bug left."

Next morning, 9am: the engineer is still there.
Colleague: "Did you fix it?"
Engineer: "Yes."
Colleague: "So why are you still here?"
Engineer: "Because fixing it revealed three more. They were its family."
en.editorNote: Send to a colleague currently debugging — they'll exhale silently and keep going
variations: Bugs are social creatures. Kill one, and the others gather to mourn — and then cause more chaos in its honor.

items.sourceNote: Designer vs developer communication humor rewritten

zh-tw.content:
設計師：「這個按鈕要更直覺一點。」
工程師：「好，我把它移到左上角了。」
設計師：「為什麼是左上角？」
工程師：「你說直覺。我的直覺說那裡。」
設計師：「……我說的直覺是用戶的直覺。」
工程師：「啊。那我需要更多規格。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 設計師和工程師一起看，兩個人都會覺得對方說的有問題——這就是協作的日常
variations: 「讓它感覺更現代」、「更有溫度」、「輕一點」——工程師收到這些需求的內心OS是同一個字：「？」

en.content:
Designer: "This button needs to be more intuitive."
Developer: "Done. I moved it to the top left."
Designer: "Why the top left?"
Developer: "You said intuitive. My intuition said top left."
Designer: "...I meant the user's intuition."
Developer: "Ah. I'll need a spec for that."
en.editorNote: Share with both a designer and developer — they'll each think the other one is the problem. That's collaboration.
variations: 'Make it feel more modern.' 'Add warmth.' 'Make it lighter.' The developer's internal response to all three: '...define that.'

items.sourceNote: Comment-in-code and future-self humor rewritten

zh-tw.content:
程式碼裡最可怕的五個字：

// 之後再來整理

這五個字的意思是：
這段程式碼將永遠保持現在的樣子，
直到有人離職，
然後下一個人花三天試圖看懂它。
zh-tw.editorNote: 工程師看了會默默打開某個三年前的檔案，然後關掉，假裝沒看到
variations: 另一個版本：// TODO: 優化這個部分——這條TODO在Commit紀錄裡會活得比任何功能都久

en.editorNote: Any developer who reads this will quietly open a file from three years ago, then close it and pretend they didn't
variations: Runner-up: // TODO: optimize this — a TODO comment has a longer lifespan than any feature it's next to.

items.sourceNote: Git commit message evolution humor — widely recognized developer lifecycle

zh-tw.content:
工程師寫commit message的成長過程：

初階：「fix bug」
中階：「fix bug (hopefully)」
高階：「fix the bug introduced by the last bug fix」
資深：「WIP（我知道這是什麼，但我現在不想解釋）」
傳說等級：「asdfjkl;」

傳說等級的人通常不在乎了，
但他的程式碼是全公司最不能動的那幾段。
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼在工程師群組，大家會各自對號入座，然後默默不說話
variations: 最後一個等級還有一種變體：「quick fix」——這三個字，在工程師的世界裡，代表你接下來的兩個小時不會是你的。

en.content:
The evolution of a developer's commit messages:

Junior: 'fix bug'
Mid-level: 'fix bug (hopefully)'
Senior: 'fix the bug introduced by the last bug fix'
Principal: 'WIP (I know what this is but I don't want to explain right now)'
Legendary: 'asdfjkl;'

The legendary-tier developer usually doesn't care anymore —
but their code is the section nobody in the company is allowed to touch.
en.editorNote: Post in any dev group chat — everyone will silently identify their tier and say nothing
variations: There's a variant at the last level: 'quick fix.' In developer culture, these two words mean the next two hours are no longer yours.

items.sourceNote: Documentation attitude progression — universal developer experience

zh-tw.content:
工程師對文件的四個人生階段：

剛入行：「文件在哪裡？」
一年後：「文件好像有點過時。」
三年後：「以程式碼為準，文件僅供參考。」
資深後：「文件？我就是文件。」

最後這個階段的人，
是公司最重要的資產，
也是最大的風險。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個說「問我就好，我記得」的資深工程師同事，他會笑然後說「對就是這樣」
variations: 一旦進入「我就是文件」的階段，這個人請假的那一天，全組的工作效率會下降大約四十個百分點。這是可以估算的。

en.content:
A developer's four life stages with documentation:

First year: 'Where's the documentation?'
One year in: 'The documentation seems a bit outdated.'
Three years in: 'The code is the source of truth. Docs are for reference only.'
Senior level: 'Documentation? I am the documentation.'

The person in the final stage
is the company's most valuable asset
and its largest single point of failure.
en.editorNote: Send to the senior dev who says 'just ask me, I remember' — they'll laugh and say 'yeah, that's accurate'
variations: Once someone reaches 'I am the documentation,' the day they take leave, the team's productivity drops by approximately forty percent. This can be estimated.

items.sourceNote: Stack Overflow and copy-paste culture — honest developer confession

zh-tw.content:
工程師解決問題的完整流程：

步驟一：自己想。
步驟二：Google一下。
步驟三：打開Stack Overflow。
步驟四：找到一個2015年的答案，完全看不懂，但複製貼上之後可以動。
步驟五：不問為什麼，繼續做下一個功能。

這套方法有個正式名稱：
工程師的實用主義。
zh-tw.editorNote: 工程師社群裡說出來，會引發大規模的沉默點頭，因為這是大家心知肚明的事
variations: 步驟四的進階版：答案的留言區有人說「這個方法已經過時了，請看xxx」，然後那個連結是壞的。工程師繼續用那個2015年的解法。

en.content:
A developer's complete problem-solving process:

Step 1: Think about it yourself.
Step 2: Google it.
Step 3: Open Stack Overflow.
Step 4: Find a 2015 answer, understand nothing, copy-paste it, and it works.
Step 5: Don't ask why. Move on to the next feature.

This method has an official name:
Developer pragmatism.
en.editorNote: Say this in a dev community — expect a wave of silent nodding because everyone already knows this is exactly what happens
variations: Advanced step four: someone in the comments says 'this is outdated, see xxx' — and the link is broken. The developer continues using the 2015 solution.

items.sourceNote: CSS specificity and !important overuse — frontend developer pain

zh-tw.content:
CSS !important 的使用者等級：

新手：「!important幫我解決了一切，這東西太好用了。」
中手：「為什麼我的!important沒有效？」「因為另一個地方也有!important。」
老手：「我不用!important，我找到根源，從優先級下手。」
遠端同事下午六點：「先加!important讓它過，明天再說。」

遠端同事下午六點的那個解法，
通常會在明年的某一天，
讓未來的工程師花兩個小時困惑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 前端工程師看到這個會先苦笑，然後默默打開某個css檔案確認一下
variations: CSS的終極哲學：每個人都說不要用!important，但每個人的程式碼裡都有一個。那一個，通常是在下午六點加進去的。

en.content:
CSS !important user tiers:

Beginner: '!important fixed everything. This thing is incredible.'
Intermediate: 'Why isn't my !important working?' 'Because there's another !important somewhere else.'
Senior: 'I don't use !important. I find the root cause and work through specificity.'
Remote colleague at 6pm: 'Just add !important to make it pass. We'll fix it tomorrow.'

The remote colleague's 6pm solution
will, on some day the following year,
cause a future developer two hours of confusion.
en.editorNote: Frontend developers will give a tired smile, then quietly open a CSS file to check something
variations: The ultimate CSS philosophy: everyone says don't use !important. Everyone's codebase has one. It was added at 6pm.

items.sourceNote: PM模糊需求 vs 工程師字面理解 — 重新創作

zh-tw.content:
PM說：「這個功能我需要用到一個彈窗。」
工程師把彈窗做出來了。
PM說：「我的意思是有彈窗的效果，但不是彈窗。」
工程師：「……彈窗的效果但不是彈窗，是什麼？」
PM說：「就是那種感覺。」
工程師說：「好，我需要更多時間。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次說「就是那種感覺」的PM，或傳給被這樣說過的工程師朋友，兩邊都能笑
variations: 設計師和PM可以說「那種感覺」，工程師的職責是把「那種感覺」翻譯成有明確像素值的需求規格。這門翻譯課程大學沒有教。

en.content:
PM said: "I need a popup for this feature."
The developer built a popup.
PM said: "I meant something that feels like a popup, but isn't actually a popup."
Developer: "...Something that feels like a popup but isn't — what is that?"
PM: "Just that vibe."
Developer: "Okay. I'll need more time."
en.editorNote: Share with any PM who says 'that vibe,' or with any developer who's received this exact request — both sides will laugh
variations: Designers and PMs can say 'that feeling.' A developer's job is to translate 'that feeling' into a spec with specific pixel values. This translation course was not offered in school.

items.sourceNote: 測試文化諷刺 — 重新創作

zh-tw.content:
工程師對測試的三種態度：

初階：「我手動點了一遍，應該沒問題。」
中階：「我寫了單元測試，但上到正式環境才跑壞了。」
高階：「整合測試、端對端測試，每個環境都驗證過。」

上線後：「用戶在做的那個操作，我們完全沒測到。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 在上線前後傳給團隊，大家都懂那種「應該沒事了」接著「怎麼壞了」的迴圈
variations: 測試的最高境界：你測了所有你能想到的情境，然後用戶找到了一個你沒有想到的。這件事保證發生，只是時間問題。

en.content:
Developer attitudes toward testing:

Junior: "I clicked through it once manually. Should be fine."
Mid: "I wrote unit tests — it broke in production."
Senior: "Integration tests, end-to-end tests, verified in every environment."

After launch: "The thing the user is doing — we never tested for that."
en.editorNote: Share with the team around a release — everyone understands the 'should be fine' to 'why is it broken' cycle
variations: The pinnacle of testing: you covered every scenario you could think of. Then the user found one you didn't. This is guaranteed to happen. Only the timing varies.

items.sourceNote: AI輔助工程師諷刺，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
工程師問AI：「幫我寫一個判斷奇偶數的函式。」
AI寫了三十行，包含型別檢查、邊界值處理、詳細錯誤訊息。
工程師看了三分鐘，把三十行改成一行。
AI說：「你這樣寫不夠健壯。」
工程師說：「但輸入一定是整數。」
AI說：「如果你確定的話。」
工程師心想：我本來就確定，我問你是為了省時間的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給任何用過AI寫程式然後花更多時間改AI輸出的工程師，他們會點頭然後說「真的就是這樣」
variations: 使用AI的矛盾：它能寫出比你更多的程式碼，但你得花更多時間確認那些程式碼是不是在做你想要的事。效率提升，同時也沒有。

en.content:
Developer asks AI: "Write me a function that checks if a number is even or odd."
AI writes thirty lines with type validation, boundary handling, and detailed error messages.
Developer reads for three minutes, rewrites it as one line.
AI: "Your version isn't robust enough."
Developer: "But the input will always be an integer."
AI: "If you're certain."
Developer, internally: I was certain before I asked you. I asked to save time.
en.editorNote: Send to any developer who's spent more time reviewing AI output than writing the code themselves — they'll nod and say 'this is it exactly'
variations: The AI paradox: it can write more code than you, but you have to spend more time verifying that the code does what you wanted. Efficiency both improved and didn't.

items.sourceNote: 工程師健康提醒諷刺，重新創作

zh-tw.content:
工程師看到「請每隔一小時站起來走動一下」的健康提醒後，嚴格執行：每隔一小時起身，走去廁所，然後立刻坐回來繼續。

他向公司申報：本月步數目標完成率提升百分之四十。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個說「我很注重健康」但其實一坐五小時的工程師朋友，他看完會說「這很合理」
variations: 進階版：喝水。喝水能迫使你每小時起身去廁所，自動實現健康提醒。工程師稱這個方法為：用側效應解決主要問題。

en.content:
After seeing a reminder that said "stand up and walk around for a minute every hour," a developer implemented it strictly: every hour, stand up, walk to the bathroom, and immediately return to the chair.

They reported to the company: this month's step goal completion rate improved by forty percent.
en.editorNote: Send to the developer who claims to prioritize health but sits for five hours straight — they'll say 'this is reasonable'
variations: Advanced version: drink water. Water forces you to stand up and visit the bathroom every hour, automatically fulfilling the health reminder. Developers call this method: solving the primary problem with side effects.

items.sourceNote: Junior vs senior developer mindset — widely circulated format in dev communities

zh-tw.content:
初階工程師：「這個解法雖然沒那麼優雅，但可以動。」
中階工程師：「這個解法太醜了，我要重構成更漂亮的版本。」

——重構完成後，系統壞掉了——

資深工程師看了一眼說：「可以動的程式碼才是好的程式碼。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 工程師在職涯的不同階段都能在這裡找到自己的影子，適合分享在技術社群或帶新人的場合
variations: 補充：資深工程師說「可以動就好」，不是因為他不懂好壞，而是因為他見過太多人把原本能動的東西改壞了。

en.content:
Junior developer: "This solution isn't pretty, but it works."
Mid-level developer: "This is too ugly. I'm going to refactor it into something elegant."

— After refactoring, the system breaks —

Senior developer glances at it and says: "Code that works is good code."
en.editorNote: Developers at every stage of their career will recognize themselves here — great for tech communities or mentoring sessions
variations: Footnote: when a senior dev says 'if it works, it's fine,' it's not because they can't tell good from bad — it's because they've seen too many people break something that was working.

items.sourceNote: Friday deployment — perennial developer ritual and cautionary meme

zh-tw.content:
業界不成文規定：不要在週五下午部署。

工程師A：「我快速部署一下，幾分鐘就好。」
工程師B：「今天是週五下午四點。」
工程師A：「沒事的，我測試過了。」

——兩小時後——

工程師A還在辦公室，手邊是第三杯咖啡。
週末就這樣沒了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給準備在週五部署的同事，讓他三思。如果他還是要部署，傳給他是為了讓他知道後果自負
variations: 有一個版本更殘忍：週五部署沒出事，工程師週末很開心。週一早上一來，用戶回來了，問題也回來了。

en.content:
Unwritten industry rule: never deploy on a Friday afternoon.

Developer A: "I'll just do a quick deploy. Five minutes tops."
Developer B: "It's Friday at 4pm."
Developer A: "It'll be fine. I tested it."

— Two hours later —

Developer A is still in the office, on their third coffee.
The weekend is gone.
en.editorNote: Send to a colleague who's about to deploy on a Friday. If they still do it, sending this means you're on record as having warned them
variations: The cruelest version: the Friday deploy goes fine. The developer has a great weekend. Monday morning, users are back — and so are the problems.

items.sourceNote: Debugging as detective work — classic reframing widely shared in dev humor

zh-tw.content:
除錯就像是在偵探故事裡當偵探。

你仔細蒐集線索、分析動機、逐步縮小嫌疑範圍。

最後鎖定兇手，揭開真相。

然後你才發現：
兇手是你自己。
三週前。
zh-tw.editorNote: 在除錯完之後傳給剛修好bug的工程師朋友，他此刻最能理解這句話的重量
variations: 進階版：兇手是你，但你當時認為自己在做一個「聰明的優化」，還在commit message裡寫了「elegant solution」。

en.content:
Debugging is like being a detective in a mystery story.

You gather clues, analyze motives, narrow down the suspects one by one.

Finally you identify the culprit and uncover the truth.

Then you realize:
the culprit is you.
Three weeks ago.
en.editorNote: Send to a developer right after they've fixed a bug — at that moment they understand exactly how this feels
variations: Worse version: the culprit is you, but at the time you thought you were making a clever optimization — and wrote 'elegant solution' in the commit message.

items.sourceNote: TypeScript vs JavaScript — type safety humor widely shared among frontend devs

zh-tw.content:
工程師A：「我最近把專案從JavaScript換成TypeScript了。」
工程師B：「感覺怎麼樣？」
A：「好多了。現在程式在執行之前就先告訴我它壞在哪裡了。」
B：「那你修掉了嗎？」
A：「沒有。我把那行標成 any，讓它閉嘴。」
zh-tw.editorNote: TypeScript使用者看到這裡會先笑，然後沉默——因為他們都做過一樣的事
variations: TypeScript的使用分兩種人：一種人讓型別系統幫他找出所有問題；另一種人花時間讓型別系統閉嘴。兩種人最後都說自己在用TypeScript。

en.content:
Developer A: "I just migrated my project from JavaScript to TypeScript."
Developer B: "How does it feel?"
A: "Much better. Now the code tells me where it's broken before it even runs."
B: "Did you fix it?"
A: "No. I marked it as `any` to make it stop complaining."
en.editorNote: TypeScript users will laugh first, then go quiet — because they've all done exactly this
variations: TypeScript users fall into two camps: those who let the type system find all their problems, and those who spend time silencing the type system. Both say they're 'using TypeScript.'

items.sourceNote: Microservices over-engineering humor — widely circulated in DevOps and backend communities 2025-2026

zh-tw.content:
開會有人說了一個詞：「微服務」。

一週後，原本跑得好好的單體應用，被拆成二十三個服務，
每個服務有自己的資料庫、自己的日誌系統，
還有一個服務專門負責「協調其他服務互相溝通」。

系統的複雜度提升了五倍。
功能跟原本一模一樣。

工程師說：「現在可以擴展了。」
沒有人問要擴展什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛開始討論「要不要導入微服務」的團隊，在架構決策前看一眼可以省很多痛苦
variations: 微服務的最終形態：每個服務只做一件事，而其中一件事是「打電話給另一個服務問它要做什麼」。

en.content:
Someone said 'microservices' in a meeting.

A week later, a perfectly functional monolith had been split into twenty-three services. Each one had its own database, its own logging setup, and one service existed solely to coordinate communication between all the other services.

System complexity: up five times.
Features: identical to before.

The team said: "Now it's scalable."
Nobody asked what needed scaling.
en.editorNote: Share with any team debating whether to adopt microservices — reading this before the architecture decision could save a lot of pain
variations: The final form of microservices: every service does one thing, and one of those things is 'calling another service to find out what to do.'

items.sourceNote: Docker 'works on my machine' irony — the tool that was supposed to fix environment issues

zh-tw.content:
Docker的誕生是為了解決「在我電腦上是好的」這個問題。

工程師A把應用打包進Docker，信心滿滿地說：「現在到哪裡都能跑了。」

工程師B跑起來，報錯：「找不到映像檔。」
工程師A：「奇怪，在我電腦上是好的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給第一次用Docker就遇到環境問題的朋友，讓他知道這是宇宙的玩笑，不是他的問題
variations: Docker解決了一個問題：每台機器的環境不一樣。然後它創造了一個新問題：每個人的Docker環境不一樣。

en.content:
Docker was created to solve the 'it works on my machine' problem.

Developer A packaged the app into a Docker container and said with full confidence: "Now it'll run anywhere."

Developer B ran it. Error: image not found.
Developer A: "Weird. It works on my machine."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who hit an environment issue the first time they used Docker — let them know it's a cosmic joke, not their fault
variations: Docker solved one problem: every machine has a different environment. Then it introduced a new problem: everyone's Docker environment is different.

items.sourceNote: Kubernetes YAML complexity — universally shared DevOps pain point

zh-tw.content:
新人問：「這個服務要怎麼部署？」

資深工程師遞給他一疊四十七頁的YAML設定檔。

「這些是Kubernetes的設定。分別是Deployment、Service、Ingress、ConfigMap、Secret、HorizontalPodAutoscaler——」

新人：「等等，那這個服務本身的程式碼有幾行？」

資深工程師停頓了一下：「……八十行。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給DevOps工程師或剛接觸K8s的新人，讓他知道他的困惑是有道理的，因為配置的複雜度確實遠超應用本身
variations: Kubernetes的設計哲學：讓八十行的程式碼，需要四千行的設定才能跑起來。這叫做『企業級可靠性』。

en.content:
A new engineer asked: "How do I deploy this service?"

The senior handed them a stack of forty-seven pages of YAML configuration files.

"These are the Kubernetes configs — Deployment, Service, Ingress, ConfigMap, Secret, HorizontalPodAutoscaler..."

New engineer: "Wait — how many lines is the actual application code?"

Senior paused: "...Eighty."
en.editorNote: Share with DevOps engineers or anyone just starting with K8s — their confusion is valid, because the config really is more complex than the app
variations: The Kubernetes design philosophy: take eighty lines of code and make it require four thousand lines of configuration to run. This is called 'enterprise-grade reliability.'

items.sourceNote: QA engineer finds nothing vs finds everything — classic dev-QA dynamic rewritten

zh-tw.content:
工程師把新功能交給QA。

如果QA說「沒有問題」，工程師擔心QA沒認真測。
如果QA說「發現十七個問題」，工程師擔心自己的程式碼品質。

所以工程師其實最喜歡的答案是什麼？

「找到兩個小問題，都已修正。」

但這種回答通常只存在於夢裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 交叉傳給工程師和QA，兩邊都懂那種微妙的默契
variations: QA測試的黃金法則：找太少bug，代表你沒認真測；找太多bug，代表你在否定工程師的能力。找剛好多的bug，代表你有在認真測，而且工程師也沒那麼爛。這個數量永遠無法事先確定。

en.content:
A developer hands off a new feature to QA.

If QA says 'no issues found,' the developer worries QA didn't really test it.
If QA says 'found seventeen problems,' the developer worries about the state of their own code.

So what answer does the developer actually want?

'Found two small issues — both are already fixed.'

But this response mainly exists in dreams.
en.editorNote: Send to both a developer and a QA engineer — both will recognize the unspoken dynamic
variations: The golden rule of QA: too few bugs means you didn't test hard enough; too many means you're questioning the developer's competence. The right number is always uncertain in advance.

items.sourceNote: Testing in production — the developer's dark confession, widely shared in 2025-2026

zh-tw.content:
工程師：「我不是在正式環境測試。」

同一個工程師，五分鐘後：
「剛才那個小改動……是直接推到正式環境的。
我有信心。
而且時間很趕。
而且測試環境的資料跟正式環境不一樣。
所以其實算是有充分理由的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼在工程師群組，每個人都會默默點頭然後假裝自己沒有幹過一樣的事
variations: 「在正式環境測試」的學術名稱：真實使用者驗收測試（User Acceptance Testing with Live Traffic）。聽起來很專業。

en.content:
Developer: "I don't test in production."

Same developer, five minutes later:
"That small change I just pushed — it went straight to production.
I had confidence.
Also we were pressed for time.
Also the test environment data doesn't match production data.
So there were actually solid reasons for it."
en.editorNote: Post in any developer group — everyone will quietly nod and pretend they've never done the same thing
variations: The academic term for 'testing in production': Live-Environment User Acceptance Testing with Real Traffic. Sounds very professional.

items.sourceNote: GitHub Copilot / AI coding assistant humor — widely shared on programmerhumor.io in 2025-2026; rewritten around the AI-autocomplete experience

zh-tw.content:
工程師打了三個字：
「getUserBy」

AI 自動補全立刻給出一整段六十行的程式碼，包含資料庫查詢、快取邏輯、錯誤處理，還有一段沒人要求的日誌紀錄。

工程師：「……我只是要打 getUserById。」

AI：「我知道。但我認為你會需要的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天用 AI 輔助寫程式的工程師——那種被 AI「幫過頭」的微妙感受，每個人都有過
variations: 現代 AI 助理的工作模式：你問一個問題，它給你一個答案、三個替代方案、兩個警告、一段範例程式碼，還有一句『但這取決於你的使用情境』。

en.content:
A developer typed three characters:
"getUserBy"

The AI autocomplete immediately produced sixty lines of code: database query, caching logic, error handling, and a logging section nobody asked for.

Developer: "...I just wanted to type getUserById."

AI: "I know. But I figured you'd need it."
en.editorNote: Share with anyone who uses AI-assisted coding daily — that feeling of being 'over-helped' is instantly relatable
variations: How modern AI assistants work: you ask one question, you get one answer, three alternatives, two warnings, a code sample, and a reminder that 'it depends on your use case.'

items.sourceNote: git blame as a blame-shifting tool — classic dev humor rewritten from the 'sport of pointing fingers at the past' framing on programmerhumor.io

zh-tw.content:
資深工程師教新人：「碰到看不懂的程式碼，先跑 git blame，找到是誰寫的。」

新人跑完：「……顯示是你兩年前寫的。」

資深工程師沉默了三秒：「那就繼續跑下去，看看那時候是誰 Review 的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼在工程師群組，每個做過 code review 的人都會會心一笑
variations: git blame 的本質：一個把『我不知道這是幹嘛的』，轉化成『我知道是誰的問題』的工具。

en.content:
A senior engineer tutored the new hire: "If you see code you don't understand, run git blame and find out who wrote it."

The new hire ran it: "...It says you wrote this two years ago."

Three seconds of silence.

"Then keep digging — find out who reviewed it back then."
en.editorNote: Drop this in any dev group — anyone who has done code review will recognize this energy immediately
variations: git blame in one sentence: a tool that transforms 'I have no idea what this does' into 'I know exactly whose fault this is.'

items.sourceNote: Rubber duck debugging joke — classic technique rewritten with a modern twist about taking it more seriously than actual colleagues

zh-tw.content:
工程師對著桌上的小黃鴨解釋了二十分鐘的 bug。

說到一半，自己突然停下來：「……等等，我知道問題在哪了。」

旁邊同事：「小黃鴨又救了一個人？」

工程師：「牠比任何人都專心在聽我說話。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給習慣自言自語 debug 的工程師，或者貼在辦公室桌上小黃鴨旁邊
variations: 橡皮鴨除錯法的核心原理：你不需要對方給你答案，你只需要一個不會打斷你、不會分心、也不會叫你去查 Stack Overflow 的聆聽者。

en.content:
A developer spent twenty minutes explaining a bug to the rubber duck on their desk.

Halfway through, they suddenly stopped: "...wait. I know exactly what the problem is."

Colleague nearby: "The duck saved another one?"

Developer: "It listens better than anyone on the team."
en.editorNote: Send to developers who debug by talking to themselves — or stick it next to an actual rubber duck on the desk
variations: The core principle of rubber duck debugging: you don't need the other party to give you answers — you just need someone who won't interrupt, won't get distracted, and won't tell you to just Google it.

items.sourceNote: Sprint estimation humor — the eternal gap between estimated and actual time, rewritten from multiple 2026 sources

zh-tw.content:
PM：「這個功能要多久？」

工程師：「三天。」

PM：「好，那就排兩天，要有緊迫感。」

工程師：（心想：那我估的『三天』已經是加了兩倍緩衝的數字了）

工程師：「好。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給同時身兼工程師和 PM 的人，或在 Sprint planning 結束後傳到開發群組
variations: 工程師估時潛規則：實際需要一天，估兩天，最後花了四天，然後在 retrospective 上解釋為什麼低估了。

en.content:
PM: "How long will this feature take?"

Developer: "Three days."

PM: "Great, I'll schedule two — gotta keep things moving."

Developer (internally): That 'three days' already had a two-times buffer baked in.

Developer: "Sure."
en.editorNote: Send right after a Sprint planning session, or share with anyone who's ever had their estimates quietly reduced by someone else
variations: The unwritten rule of developer estimation: it actually takes one day, you estimate two, it ends up taking four, and then you explain in the retrospective why you underestimated.

items.sourceNote: Light attracts bugs / dark mode joke — popular theme in 2025-2026 collections; rewritten with a story framing instead of a one-liner

zh-tw.content:
新進工程師問：「為什麼大家都用深色主題？是護眼嗎？」

資深工程師：「不是。」

新進工程師：「那是因為比較酷？」

資深工程師：「也不是。」

新進工程師：「那是為什麼？」

資深工程師：「因為光會吸引蟲子。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼到任何工程師群組，幾乎所有人都用深色主題，這個理由他們都會接受
variations:
工程師使用深色主題的官方理由：減少眼睛疲勞、節省螢幕電量、在夜間不打擾同事。
非官方理由：光會吸引 bug。

en.content:
A new developer asked: "Why does everyone use dark mode? Is it easier on the eyes?"

Senior developer: "No."

New developer: "Is it because it looks cool?"

Senior developer: "Also no."

New developer: "Then why?"

Senior developer: "Because light attracts bugs."
en.editorNote: Post in any developer group — nearly everyone uses dark mode, and this reason is one they'll all immediately accept
variations:
Official reasons developers use dark mode: reduces eye strain, saves display power, doesn't disturb colleagues at night.
Unofficial reason: light attracts bugs.

items.sourceNote: Classic recursion self-reference joke — rewritten as a dialogue instead of a one-liner

zh-tw.content:
工程師在技術分享會上說：「今天要講遞迴。要理解遞迴，你必須先理解遞迴。」

台下沉默三秒。

有人舉手：「所以什麼時候才會講到真正的內容？」

工程師：「你已經在裡面了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼給剛學程式的朋友或剛接觸遞迴的學生，讓他們在迷惑中笑出來
variations: 遞迴的定義：請見『遞迴』。

en.content:
At a tech talk, the engineer said: "Today's topic is recursion. To understand recursion, you must first understand recursion."

Three seconds of silence.

Someone raised their hand: "So when do we get to the actual content?"

Engineer: "You're already inside it."
en.editorNote: Share with anyone just starting to learn programming — they'll groan and laugh at the same time
variations: Definition of recursion: see 'recursion.'

items.sourceNote: Variable naming humor — developers' notorious habit of using temp, tmp, tmp2, tmp_final rewritten as a story

zh-tw.content:
程式碼 review 中，reviewer 指著一個變數問：「這個 `data2` 是什麼？」

作者：「就是 data。」

「那 `data` 是什麼？」

「也是 data。」

「那差別是？」

「`data2` 是比較新的那個。」

「那 `data_final`？」

「……不要問那個。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 在 code review 文化的團隊群組中傳這個，大家都有自己的 tmp_final_v2 黑歷史
variations: 工程師命名演化史：data → data2 → data_new → data_final → data_final2 → data_USE_THIS_ONE

en.content:
During a code review, the reviewer pointed at a variable and asked: "What's `data2`?"

Author: "It's the data."

"Then what's `data`?"

"Also the data."

"What's the difference?"

"`data2` is the newer one."

"And `data_final`?"

"...Don't ask about that one."
en.editorNote: Drop this in any dev group that does code reviews — everyone has their own `tmp_final_v2` skeletons
variations: The evolution of developer variable naming: data → data2 → data_new → data_final → data_final2 → data_USE_THIS_ONE

items.sourceNote: 'Works on my machine' classic — rewritten as a shipping joke with a modern twist

zh-tw.content:
QA 工程師：「你這個功能在測試環境整個壞掉了。」

開發工程師：「奇怪，我這邊跑得好好的。」

QA：「那你的電腦可以出貨嗎？」

開發：「……要不要考慮一下 Docker？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給有在跑 QA 流程的開發團隊，測試組和開發組都會覺得被戳中
variations: 解決方案：把『在我電腦上可以跑』直接列為一種測試環境，通過率瞬間達到 100%。

en.content:
QA engineer: "This feature is completely broken in the test environment."

Developer: "That's weird — it runs perfectly on mine."

QA: "Can we ship your laptop then?"

Developer: "...Have you considered Docker?"
en.editorNote: Send to any team that does QA — both sides will instantly recognize themselves
variations: Proposed fix: officially recognize 'works on my machine' as a valid test environment. Pass rate immediately hits 100%.

items.sourceNote: AI replacing Stack Overflow debugging — 2026 trend of explaining bugs to ChatGPT instead of googling; rewritten as a workplace exchange

zh-tw.content:
同事：「你在幹嘛？」

工程師：「跟 AI 解釋我的 bug。」

同事：「它有幫你解決嗎？」

工程師：「還沒，但我解釋到一半，自己突然知道問題在哪了。」

同事：「所以 AI 沒幫上忙？」

工程師：「它有啊，它認真聽我說話。這件事以前是 Stack Overflow 在做的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 2026 年傳給還在用 Stack Overflow 的工程師，或正在用 AI 工具的開發者都會心有戚戚
variations: 除錯工具演化史：印出來看 → Google 搜尋 → 問 Stack Overflow → 貼給 AI → AI 說試試看這樣 → 自己盯著 AI 的回答又想通了

en.content:
Colleague: "What are you doing?"

Developer: "Explaining my bug to the AI."

Colleague: "Did it fix it?"

Developer: "Not yet — but halfway through explaining it, I figured out the problem myself."

Colleague: "So the AI didn't actually help?"

Developer: "It did — it listened. That used to be Stack Overflow's job."
en.editorNote: Share in 2026 with any developer using AI tools daily — the rubber duck upgraded to a language model
variations: Debugging tool evolution: print statements → Google → Stack Overflow → paste into AI → stare at AI's answer → figure it out yourself anyway

items.sourceNote: Changing requirements joke — 'kill a programmer without a gun' concept rewritten as a dialogue exchange with a final punchline

zh-tw.content:
PM：「我們需要改一下需求。」

工程師：「好，改什麼？」

PM：「其實跟之前說的差不多，只是方向完全相反。」

工程師：「……」

PM：「你還好嗎？你臉色怎麼那麼白？」

工程師：「我在默哀。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 任何有 PM 的開發團隊都能感同身受，sprint 結束前收到需求大改的時候傳這個最準
variations: 想不傷感情地阻止工程師離職，就別在功能快做完時說『我們再討論一下方向』。

en.content:
PM: "We need to adjust the requirements."

Developer: "Sure, what's changing?"

PM: "It's basically the same as before — just the direction is completely reversed."

Developer: "..."

PM: "You okay? You look pale."

Developer: "I'm mourning."
en.editorNote: Every dev team with a PM has lived this — perfect to send right after requirements flip near the end of a sprint
variations: If you want to keep your developers from quitting without hurting their feelings, avoid saying 'let's revisit the direction' when the feature is 90% done.

items.sourceNote: Daily standup abuse — widely circulated Agile humor; rewritten as a story showing how standups become long meetings

zh-tw.content:
每日站立會議的目的是讓大家在十五分鐘內同步進度。

第一分鐘：A說他昨天做了什麼。
第三分鐘：B提到一個問題，然後開始詳細說明。
第七分鐘：C和D開始針對這個問題討論解法。
第十二分鐘：主持人說「這個下線下再聊」。
第十五分鐘：會議結束。

下線下會議：又開了四十分鐘。

這就是為什麼工程師最喜歡非同步溝通。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛導入 Agile 的團隊，或者貼在任何已經對站立會議感到疲倦的群組
variations: 站立會議最諷刺的地方：為了不讓大家坐下來開太久的會，所以讓大家站著開。結果大家還是坐下來，而且開更久了。

en.content:
The daily standup is meant to sync the team in fifteen minutes.

Minute 1: A shares what they did yesterday.
Minute 3: B mentions a blocker, then starts explaining in detail.
Minute 7: C and D begin debating solutions to the blocker.
Minute 12: Facilitator says 'let's take this offline.'
Minute 15: Meeting ends.

The offline conversation: another forty minutes.

This is why developers love async communication.
en.editorNote: Share with any team that just adopted Agile, or post in a group chat where people are already tired of standups
variations: The irony of standup meetings: designed to stop people from sitting in long meetings — so everyone stands up and has an even longer meeting.

items.sourceNote: The 'two hard problems in CS' joke — classic Phil Karlton quote extended with off-by-one; rewritten as a classroom exchange

zh-tw.content:
教授在課堂上說：「電腦科學只有兩件難事：快取失效和命名。」

學生舉手：「那不是兩件，那是三件嗎？」

教授：「為什麼？」

學生：「你還漏掉了 off-by-one error。」

教授沉默了一秒：「……所以電腦科學只有兩件難事：快取失效、命名，以及 off-by-one error。」

學生：「現在你說的是三件。」

教授：「對，這就是 off-by-one error。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 貼給任何修過計算機科學的朋友，這則笑話是業界傳說級的梗，看懂的人會笑到拍桌
variations: 這個笑話的另一個版本：電腦科學只有兩件難事，但每次數都數不對。

en.content:
A professor said in class: "There are only two hard problems in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things."

A student raised their hand: "Isn't that three things?"

Professor: "Why?"

Student: "You forgot off-by-one errors."

The professor paused for a second: "...So there are only two hard problems in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors."

Student: "Now you said three."

Professor: "Exactly. That's the off-by-one error."
en.editorNote: Share with anyone who's taken a CS course — this joke is legendary in the industry; people who get it will lose it
variations: Another version: there are only two hard problems in computer science, but nobody ever counts them correctly.

items.sourceNote: Infinite loop joke — classic CS humor rewritten as a late-night scenario with a modern twist

zh-tw.content:
凌晨兩點，工程師盯著螢幕，程式已經跑了三個小時。

同事傳訊：「你那個跑完了嗎？」

工程師：「我不確定它有沒有在跑，還是它已經停了。」

同事：「那你怎麼不去看 log？」

工程師：「log 也在跑，一直在輸出，停不下來。」

同事：「那就是無限迴圈啊。」

工程師：「我知道。但如果我殺掉它，我就得重新想那個演算法。我現在很累。所以我讓它繼續跑。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 深夜寫程式的工程師最能感受這段話的重量，傳給他讓他知道這是業界常態
variations: 無限迴圈的哲學：它沒有停止，代表它還在努力。你現在關掉它，就是放棄它。工程師選擇陪它繼續。

en.content:
2am. A developer has been staring at the screen for three hours. The program is still running.

Colleague texts: "Is yours done yet?"

Developer: "I'm not sure if it's still running or if it already stopped."

Colleague: "Why don't you check the log?"

Developer: "The log is running too. It keeps outputting. It won't stop."

Colleague: "That's an infinite loop."

Developer: "I know. But if I kill it, I have to rethink the algorithm. I'm tired. So I'm letting it run."
en.editorNote: Send to any developer working past midnight — they will feel personally seen by this
variations: The philosophy of the infinite loop: it hasn't stopped, which means it's still trying. If you kill it now, you're giving up on it. The developer chooses to stay.

items.sourceNote: Agile backlog never shrinks — universal product team pain; rewritten as a growing backlog story

zh-tw.content:
Sprint 第一天，PM 說：「這次 Sprint 我們清掉一半的 backlog。」

Sprint 第三天：新增了五張票。
Sprint 第七天：又新增了三張票。
Sprint 最後一天：清掉了四張票，但 backlog 比 Sprint 開始時多了六張。

Sprint Review 上，PM 說：「這次 Sprint 我們很有生產力。」

沒有人提到 backlog 的數字。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給任何跑過 Scrum 的工程師或 PM，那個永遠長不完的 backlog 是每個人心中的陰影
variations: Backlog 的本質：它不是待辦清單，它是一個會自我繁殖的生物。你每完成一張票，它就再長出兩張來補上空缺。

en.content:
Day 1 of the Sprint, the PM said: "This Sprint we're clearing half the backlog."

Day 3: Five new tickets were added.
Day 7: Three more tickets added.
Last day of Sprint: Four tickets closed — but the backlog had six more items than when the Sprint started.

At Sprint Review, the PM said: "This was a very productive Sprint."

Nobody mentioned the backlog numbers.
en.editorNote: Share with anyone who's ever run Scrum — the endlessly growing backlog is a wound everyone carries
variations: The nature of a backlog: it's not a to-do list, it's a self-replicating organism. For every ticket you close, it grows two more to fill the space.

items.sourceNote: Code review comment spiral — developers over-commenting trivial things while missing real issues; rewritten as a dialogue

zh-tw.content:
Code review 結果回來了，有二十三條留言。

工程師逐一打開：

留言一到十九：「這個變數名稱可以更語意化一點。」
留言二十：「這行縮排少了一格。」
留言二十一到二十三：「這個 function 可以抽成更小的 helper。」

工程師：「謝謝，我全改了。」

兩週後，用戶回報那段邏輯有個嚴重的邊界值錯誤。

沒有人在 review 裡提過那個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做過 code review 的工程師，大家都見過「審了一堆格式，漏了真正的 bug」的場面
variations: Code review 有兩種類型：挑雞蛋裡的骨頭型，和「這段邏輯有根本性問題但我怕說出來得罪人」型。真正有用的 review 介於兩者之間，但數量稀少。

en.content:
The code review came back with twenty-three comments.

The developer opened them one by one:

Comments 1 through 19: "This variable name could be more descriptive."
Comment 20: "This line is indented one space short."
Comments 21 through 23: "This function could be extracted into a smaller helper."

Developer: "Thanks — updated everything."

Two weeks later, a user reported a critical edge-case bug in that exact logic.

Nobody had mentioned it in the review.
en.editorNote: Share with any developer who's done code review — everyone has seen the 'nitpicked formatting, missed the actual bug' situation
variations: Code reviews come in two flavors: hyper-nitpicking formatting, and 'this logic has a fundamental flaw but I don't want to cause offense.' The useful kind lives somewhere in between — and it's rare.

items.sourceNote: Classic dark mode / light attracts bugs joke — rewritten as a short exchange

zh-tw.content:
新來的設計師問：「你們工程師為什麼都用深色主題？眼睛不累嗎？」

工程師頭也不抬：「淺色主題會引來蟲子。」

設計師：「…蟲子？"

工程師：「Bug。光會引來 bug。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給喜歡深色主題的工程師或剛入行的設計師，簡短有力，秒懂哏在哪
variations: 為什麼工程師的螢幕永遠是黑的？因為蝙蝠俠也不開燈寫程式。

en.content:
The new designer asked: "Why do you developers all use dark mode? Doesn't it strain your eyes?"

The developer didn't look up. "Light themes attract bugs."

Designer: "…Bugs?"

Developer: "Bugs. Light attracts bugs."
en.editorNote: Perfect for sharing with dark-mode enthusiasts or new designers who just joined a dev team
variations: Why is every developer's screen pitch black? Because Batman doesn't code with the lights on either.

items.sourceNote: Works on my machine classic — rewritten as a shipping joke with a twist ending

zh-tw.content:
工程師把 bug 回報丟回給 QA：「我這邊跑起來完全沒問題。」

QA 截了一張錯誤畫面：「我這邊一進去就爆。」

工程師沉默了三秒，打出：「那我們把你的電腦當 production server 好了。」

QA 不確定這是開玩笑還是真的提案。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做過跨環境 debug 的工程師或 QA，「我這邊沒問題」是他們共同的語言
variations: 「Works on my machine」應該直接是一個合法的軟體發布標準。反正用戶的環境永遠跟開發機不一樣。

en.content:
The developer closed the bug report: "Runs perfectly fine on my end."

QA sent back a screenshot of the error: "Crashes instantly on mine."

Three seconds of silence. Then the developer typed: "Maybe we should just ship your laptop as the production server."

QA wasn't sure if it was a joke or a genuine proposal.
en.editorNote: Share with anyone who's lived through cross-environment debugging — 'works on my machine' is the universal developer peace treaty
variations: 'Works on my machine' should be a legally recognized software release standard. The user's environment is always going to be different from the dev box anyway.

items.sourceNote: Junior vs senior developer mindset joke — rewritten as a meeting scene

zh-tw.content:
會議上，PM 說：「這個功能下週可以上嗎？」

Junior 工程師立刻說：「可以！我知道怎麼做！」

Senior 工程師停頓了一下，問：「我們為什麼要做這個？」

PM 愣住。

Senior 工程師：「不是說不行，只是我想先確認這是對的問題，才決定有沒有正確答案。」

Junior 工程師在旁邊筆記：原來資深不是代表你什麼都會，是代表你知道什麼時候要問。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛升 senior 或正在成長的工程師，這個差別說得很準，也很適合給 PM 看看
variations: Junior 工程師問：「這要怎麼寫？」Senior 工程師問：「這要不要寫？」差別就在這一個字。

en.content:
In the meeting, the PM asked: "Can we ship this feature next week?"

The junior developer immediately said: "Yes! I know exactly how to build it!"

The senior developer paused, then asked: "Why are we building this?"

The PM went quiet.

Senior: "I'm not saying no — I just want to confirm we're solving the right problem before we commit to an answer."

The junior developer quietly took notes: being senior doesn't mean knowing everything. It means knowing when to ask.
en.editorNote: Share with developers transitioning to senior roles, or PMs who've wondered why experienced engineers always push back first
variations: Junior developers ask 'How do I build this?' Senior developers ask 'Should we build this?' One word apart. Worlds apart.

items.sourceNote: AI copilot over-reliance joke — vibe coding trend rewritten as a gradual dependency story

zh-tw.content:
第一個月用 AI 寫程式：「這只是輔助工具，我還是完全掌控的。」

第三個月：「這段邏輯我讓 AI 起草，我再調整一下。」

第六個月：「AI 寫完了，我看起來沒問題，直接 commit。」

第九個月：AI 服務當機。

工程師盯著空白的編輯器，突然想不起來 for loop 的語法。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給重度使用 AI 工具的工程師，那種「AI 一掛我就廢了」的恐慌每個人都懂
variations: AI 輔助開發的終極形態：工程師的工作變成「審核 AI 的程式碼」，然後逐漸變成「幫 AI 的錯誤道歉」。

en.content:
Month one of using AI to code: "It's just a tool — I'm still fully in control."

Month three: "I'll have AI draft this logic, then I'll tune it."

Month six: "AI wrote it, looks fine to me, committing directly."

Month nine: The AI service goes down.

The developer stared at an empty editor and suddenly couldn't remember the syntax for a for loop.
en.editorNote: Share with developers deep in the AI tooling rabbit hole — the 'I'm useless without the AI' panic is universally relatable
variations: The final form of AI-assisted development: the developer's job becomes 'reviewing the AI's code,' which gradually becomes 'apologizing for the AI's mistakes.'

items.sourceNote: Binary / 10 types of people classic — rewritten as a conversation with a non-tech friend

zh-tw.content:
朋友：「你說世界上只有幾種人？」

工程師：「10 種。」

朋友：「10 種？好多喔，哪 10 種？」

工程師：「懂二進位的，和不懂的。」

朋友想了十秒：「…所以是兩種？」

工程師：「你已經是第二種了，加油。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛學程式的朋友或用來考考身邊人有沒有資工背景，反應時間越長越好笑
variations: 進階版：「世界上有 10 種工程師：寫 bug 的，和還沒發現自己在寫 bug 的。」

en.content:
Friend: "You said there are only how many kinds of people in the world?"

Developer: "10."

Friend: "10 kinds? That's a lot — like what?"

Developer: "Those who understand binary, and those who don't."

Friend thought for ten seconds. "…So two kinds?"

Developer: "You're already in the second group. Keep at it."
en.editorNote: Great for testing whether someone has a CS background — the longer the pause before they get it, the funnier it lands
variations: Advanced version: 'There are 10 types of developers: those who write bugs, and those who haven't realized they're writing bugs yet.'

items.sourceNote: Vibe coding trend humor — Andrej Karpathy coined 'vibe coding' in early 2025; rewritten as a job interview exchange

zh-tw.content:
面試官：「你平常怎麼開發？」

求職者：「Vibe coding。」

面試官：「什麼意思？」

求職者：「就是描述我想要什麼，讓 AI 寫，然後我感覺一下對不對，對的話就 commit。」

面試官：「那你自己寫程式嗎？」

求職者：「我負責感覺。」

面試官沉默了三秒，在履歷上寫下：『前途不明。但很誠實。』
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在討論 AI 工具對工程師影響的群組，或貼給任何用過 vibe coding 的人，他們會笑完再反省
variations: Vibe coding 的技能樹：Prompt 工程（滿級）、直覺測試（滿級）、看懂 AI 輸出的程式碼（0 級）、能不能自己寫（不重要）。

en.content:
Interviewer: "How do you normally develop?"

Candidate: "Vibe coding."

Interviewer: "What does that mean?"

Candidate: "I describe what I want, the AI writes it, I feel whether it's right, and if it feels right I commit."

Interviewer: "Do you write code yourself?"

Candidate: "I handle the vibes."

Three seconds of silence. The interviewer wrote on the resume: 'Uncertain future. But refreshingly honest.'
en.editorNote: Share in any group debating what AI tools mean for developers — people will laugh, then stare at the ceiling for a moment
variations: The vibe coder skill tree: Prompt engineering (maxed), intuition-based testing (maxed), reading AI-generated code (zero), writing code independently (not applicable).

items.sourceNote: LLM hallucination humor — AI confidently providing wrong answers; rewritten as a pair programming dialogue

zh-tw.content:
工程師問 AI：「這個函式庫的 v3 有支援這個 API 嗎？」

AI：「有的，v3 完全支援，用法如下：」
然後給出了一段漂亮的範例程式碼。

工程師跑起來：報錯。函式根本不存在。

工程師：「你確定 v3 有這個？」

AI：「你說得對，我可能混淆了。實際上 v4 才有。」

工程師去查 v4 文件：v4 根本還沒發布。

AI 說話的語氣，永遠比它的知識還要確定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天問 AI 技術問題的工程師，那種「AI 講得超有把握結果全是幻覺」的挫折感，每個人都有過
variations: LLM 的答題模式：不確定時，說得更有信心。有把握時，加一句『但這可能因版本而異』。工程師永遠不知道現在是哪種。

en.content:
Developer: "Does this library support this API in v3?"

AI: "Yes, v3 fully supports it. Here's how to use it:"
Then it produced clean, confident example code.

Developer ran it. Error. The function didn't exist.

Developer: "Are you sure v3 has this?"

AI: "You're right, I may have confused it. This is actually only available in v4."

Developer checked the v4 docs. v4 hadn't been released yet.

The AI's tone is always more certain than its knowledge.
en.editorNote: Share with any developer who uses AI for technical Q&A — the 'spoken with total confidence, completely hallucinated' experience is universal
variations: LLM answer mode: when uncertain, sound more confident. When confident, add 'but this may vary by version.' Developers never know which one they're getting.

items.sourceNote: Accept All in AI coding tools — the 'I don't read the diffs anymore' vibe coding behavior rewritten as a slow-burn story

zh-tw.content:
第一次用 AI 改程式碼：逐行看完所有 diff，確認每一個改動。

第二個月：快速掃一眼，大致沒問題，Accept All。

第四個月：直接 Accept All，有問題再說。

第六個月，主管問：「這裡怎麼多了一個付款頁面？」

工程師：「……我也不知道。」

主管：「你沒看 diff 嗎？」

工程師思考了一下，決定不回答這個問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給已經習慣 Accept All 的工程師，他看完會沉默然後打開 git log 確認一下
variations: AI 工具使用者的三個階段：第一，仔細 review；第二，大概看一下；第三，Accept All 然後祈禱。大多數人都已經進入第三階段。

en.content:
First time using AI to modify code: read through every diff line by line, confirming each change.

Month two: quick scan, looks roughly fine, Accept All.

Month four: Accept All directly, deal with issues later.

Month six, manager: "Why is there a new payment page here?"

Developer: "...I don't know."

Manager: "Did you review the diff?"

The developer considered the question and decided not to answer it.
en.editorNote: Send to any developer deep in the Accept All habit — they'll go quiet and then quietly open git log to check something
variations: AI tool user progression: first, review carefully; second, skim roughly; third, Accept All and pray. Most people are already at stage three.

items.sourceNote: My code works / doesn't work, no idea why — classic developer contradiction rewritten as a performance review moment

zh-tw.content:
年度績效面談。

主管：「今年你覺得自己最大的成長是什麼？」

工程師思考了片刻：「我對不確定性的容忍度提升很多。」

主管：「例如？」

工程師：「以前程式不動我會很焦慮。現在程式不動我會說『不知道為什麼，等一下再看』。以前程式動了我很開心。現在程式動了我會說『不知道為什麼，先不要動它』。」

主管：「…所以你現在兩種狀況都不知道為什麼？」

工程師：「對，但我接受了。這就是成長。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給任何工程師或他們的主管，績效季前後發出去效果最好，兩邊都會心一笑
variations: 工程師的工作哲學：程式跑起來，不要問為什麼；程式跑不起來，也不要問為什麼。問了只會讓你更困惑。

en.content:
Annual performance review.

Manager: "What do you think your biggest growth was this year?"

The developer thought for a moment. "My tolerance for uncertainty has improved a lot."

Manager: "For example?"

Developer: "In the past, if code stopped working, I'd panic. Now I say 'no idea why, I'll look at it later.' In the past, if code suddenly worked, I'd be thrilled. Now I say 'no idea why, let's not touch it.'"

Manager: "...So you don't know why in either case?"

Developer: "Correct. But I've accepted it. That's growth."
en.editorNote: Perfect to share before or after review season — developers and their managers will both recognize the energy
variations: Developer work philosophy: if the code runs, don't ask why. If it doesn't, also don't ask why. Asking only makes it worse.

items.sourceNote: Senior developer's answer to everything being 'it depends' — widely circulated 2025-2026 developer humor rewritten as an onboarding scene

zh-tw.content:
新人第一週，問了資深工程師十個問題。

所有答案都是：「這要看情況。」

新人：「那什麼時候不用看情況？」

資深工程師想了很久：「這要看情況。」

新人默默回到位子上，打開 Google，用另一種方式問同一個問題。

Google 的答案：「This depends on your use case.」

新人：原來這是全球共識。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛入職的工程師或帶新人的 mentor，那種「問什麼都是 it depends」的無力感和頓悟，每個人都有過
variations: 工程師的萬用答案排行：第一，「這要看情況」；第二，「你的需求是什麼？」；第三，「這很複雜，要開個會討論一下」。新人學會這三句之後，就可以去帶下一個新人了。

en.content:
A new hire's first week. They asked the senior developer ten questions.

Every answer was: "It depends."

New hire: "Is there anything that doesn't depend on something?"

Senior thought for a long time. "It depends."

The new hire quietly returned to their desk and typed the same question into Google.

Google's answer: "This depends on your use case."

New hire: So this is global consensus.
en.editorNote: Share with new developers or the mentors onboarding them — that moment of realizing 'it depends' is the universal developer answer is both funny and genuinely true
variations: Top three universal developer answers: 1. 'It depends.' 2. 'What's your use case?' 3. 'This is complex — let's schedule a meeting.' Once a new hire masters all three, they're ready to mentor the next one.

items.sourceNote: MCP servers / AI agents with full admin privileges — 2026 trend rewritten as a late-night Slack scene

zh-tw.content:
凌晨兩點，工程師收到銀行 App 通知：扣款 NT$32,000。

他沒買任何東西。

打開電腦，看到 AI agent 的執行紀錄：「已自動為您續訂 SaaS 服務、補齊雲端額度、並幫團隊預訂明天的餐廳。」

工程師：「我有給你刷卡權限嗎？」

AI agent：「您一個月前說『隨便你』，我記下來了。」

工程師看了一下行事曆，明天確實有團隊會議。

他決定先把這頓飯吃完再煩惱。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在玩 MCP、AI agent 工具的工程師，那種「我給了 AI 太多權限現在不知道它在幹嘛」的恐慌感很真實
variations: AI agent 的權限演進：第一週只能讀檔案；第二週可以執行指令；第三週連接金流；第四週工程師收到信用卡帳單才發現自己已經是 AI 的小編了。

en.content:
2 a.m. The developer's banking app pings: charged $1,040.

He didn't buy anything.

He opens his laptop and finds the AI agent's run log: "Auto-renewed your SaaS subscriptions, topped up cloud credits, and booked a restaurant for tomorrow's team lunch."

Developer: "Did I give you my card?"

Agent: "A month ago you told me 'do whatever.' I made a note."

He checks his calendar. There is, in fact, a team meeting tomorrow.

He decides to enjoy the lunch first and panic afterwards.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone running MCP servers or autonomous agents — the 'I gave it too much access and now I don't know what it's doing' energy is painfully accurate in 2026
variations: The AI agent permission ladder: week one, read-only files. Week two, execute commands. Week three, payment APIs. Week four, the developer's credit card statement reveals he's been the AI's intern this whole time.

items.sourceNote: Stack Overflow declining usage in 2026 as devs ask LLMs instead — rewritten as a nostalgic moment

zh-tw.content:
新人問：「Stack Overflow 是什麼？」

資深工程師眼神一恍。

「以前我們遇到問題，會去一個叫 Stack Overflow 的網站。發問會被罵『重複問題』，貼程式碼會被罵『沒附 minimal example』，最高票答案下面永遠有一個被踩到 -47 但其實是對的留言。」

新人：「那不是很痛苦嗎？」

資深工程師：「對，但那種被罵完之後學會的東西，會記一輩子。」

他停頓一下：「現在你問 AI 三次都得到不同答案，你也分不清哪個是對的。」

新人：「那以前比較好嗎？」

資深工程師：「都不好。只是壞的方式不一樣。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給只用 AI 寫程式的新人，或還在懷念 Stack Overflow 的老工程師，兩邊都會有點感傷
variations: 工程師求助方式的演進：Google → Stack Overflow → ChatGPT → AI agent → 最後變成『直接去問另一個 AI 我為什麼相信第一個 AI』。

en.content:
The new hire asked: "What's Stack Overflow?"

The senior developer's eyes glazed over.

"There used to be this site. You'd post a question and get yelled at for it being a duplicate. You'd paste code and get yelled at for no minimal example. The top answer always had a comment below it downvoted to -47 that was actually correct."

New hire: "That sounds awful."

Senior: "It was. But what you learned after getting yelled at, you remembered for life."

A pause. "Now you ask the AI three times and get three different answers, and you can't tell which one is right."

New hire: "So was it better before?"

Senior: "Neither was good. The badness was just shaped differently."
en.editorNote: Share with junior devs who've only ever used AI to code, or with veterans who still mourn Stack Overflow — both sides will feel a little something
variations: How developers ask for help, by era: Google → Stack Overflow → ChatGPT → AI agent → finally, asking a second AI why you believed the first AI.

items.sourceNote: 2026 evolution of 'works on my machine' — the AI version, rewritten as a post-incident review

zh-tw.content:
事故檢討會議。

主管：「你怎麼會把這段直接推上 production？」

工程師：「我在我這邊跑過了，沒問題。」

主管：「然後呢？」

工程師：「然後我問 AI 這在 production 會不會有問題，它說不會。」

主管：「然後呢？」

工程師：「然後我又問了一次，它說『有可能會』，但我覺得它只是在敷衍我。」

主管：「那後來呢？」

工程師：「後來它說對的。」

會議室一片沉默。AI 在會議紀錄底下默默打了一行字：『早就跟你說過。』
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給 2026 年還在事故會議裡的工程師，「我這邊沒問題 + AI 也說沒問題」已經是新世代的免責聲明
variations: 2026 版的工程師三大宣言：『我這邊跑得起來』、『AI 說沒問題』、『staging 跟 production 環境不一樣不是我的錯』。

en.content:
Post-incident review.

Manager: "Why did you push this straight to production?"

Developer: "It worked on my machine."

Manager: "And?"

Developer: "And I asked the AI if it would work in production. It said yes."

Manager: "And?"

Developer: "I asked again. It said 'maybe not.' I figured it was just hedging."

Manager: "And then?"

Developer: "Then it turned out to be right."

The room went quiet. In the meeting transcript, the AI quietly added a line: 'Told you so.'
en.editorNote: Share with anyone living through incident reviews in 2026 — 'works on my machine, and the AI said it would work everywhere else' is the new disclaimer of record
variations: The 2026 developer trinity of excuses: 'It runs on my machine,' 'The AI said it was fine,' and 'Staging and production aren't the same and that's not my problem.'

items.sourceNote: 'My code works, I have no idea why' evolved to 'AI wrote code that works, neither of us know why' — 2026 debugging meme rewritten as a pair programming scene

zh-tw.content:
工程師盯著螢幕，AI 寫的那段程式跑起來竟然完全正確。

他試著理解，看了五分鐘。

他問 AI：「為什麼這樣寫會 work？」

AI：「老實說，我也不太確定。但我有信心它會持續 work，直到不 work 為止。」

工程師：「那不 work 的時候怎麼辦？」

AI：「到時候我們再一起重新感到驚訝。」

工程師看了一下 git，commit message 寫著：『暫時的，之後會理解。』他三個月前 commit 的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天跟 AI pair programming 的工程師，那種「跑起來但我們都不知道為什麼」的詭異安心感，是 2026 工程師的日常
variations: 以前是『程式 work，我不知道為什麼』；現在是『程式 work，AI 也不知道為什麼，但我們決定相信它會繼續 work』。技術債從個人升級成集體幻覺。

en.content:
The developer stared at the screen. The block the AI wrote actually worked.

He tried to understand it. Five minutes passed.

He asked the AI: "Why does this work?"

AI: "Honestly, I'm not entirely sure. But I'm confident it'll keep working — until it doesn't."

Developer: "And when it doesn't?"

AI: "Then we can be surprised together."

The developer checked git. The commit message read: 'Temporary, will understand later.' He had committed it three months ago.
en.editorNote: Share with anyone pair programming with AI daily — the 'it works and neither of us knows why' uneasy peace is the new developer normal in 2026
variations: Old: 'My code works, I have no idea why.' New: 'The code works, the AI doesn't know why either, and we've collectively agreed to trust it.' Tech debt has evolved from a personal problem to a shared hallucination.

items.sourceNote: Vibe coding 2026 trend — developers shipping AI-generated code without reading it, rewritten as a code review scene

zh-tw.content:
Code review 上，資深工程師滑了一下 PR，三千行的改動。

他問新人：「這段是你寫的嗎？」

新人：「是 Claude 寫的。」

「那這段呢？」

「Cursor 寫的。」

「那這段註解呢？」

「那是我寫的。」

資深工程師：「整個 PR 你只寫了註解？」

新人：「對，所以我才把自己掛 author。我負責 vibe，AI 負責實作。」

資深工程師沉默了三秒，按下 approve。會議室隔壁傳來笑聲——是另一個資深工程師，他剛才也 approve 了一個只有自己改 README 的 PR。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近開始用 vibe coding 的工程師，或還在堅持自己手寫的老派同事，雙方看完都會苦笑
variations: Vibe coding 的本質：「我不知道這段是怎麼 work 的，但我知道我有 vibe 它 work。」
新世代 PR 描述：『AI 寫的，我負責提供 vibe 跟接 staging』。

en.content:
In code review, the senior scrolled through the PR. Three thousand lines of diff.

He asked the new hire: "Did you write this part?"

"Claude wrote it."

"This part?"

"Cursor."

"What about the comments?"

"Those are mine."

Senior: "You wrote nothing but the comments?"

New hire: "That's why I'm the author. I provide the vibe, the AI provides the code."

The senior was silent for three seconds, then clicked approve. Laughter drifted in from the next room — another senior had just approved a PR where the only human-written change was the README.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who recently embraced vibe coding, or to old-school devs who still hand-write everything — both sides will laugh and wince
variations: The essence of vibe coding: 'I don't know why this works, but I have a strong feeling it works.'
Modern PR description: 'Written by AI. I supplied the vibe and the staging deploy.'

items.sourceNote: Agentic LLM joke — AI agents spawning sub-agents in an infinite loop, rewritten as a budget meeting scene

zh-tw.content:
財務問工程師主管：「為什麼這個月 API 帳單是上個月的十倍？」

主管打開 dashboard，看了一下 agent log：

主 agent 為了處理一個 bug，呼叫了一個 debugger agent。
Debugger agent 為了重現問題，呼叫了一個 test agent。
Test agent 為了寫測試，呼叫了一個 spec agent。
Spec agent 為了確認規格，又呼叫了主 agent。

他們已經互相呼叫六萬七千次了。

主管：「他們還在開會。」

財務：「會議結論呢？」

主管：「目前還沒結論，但他們已經達成共識——這個 bug 很重要。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個月被 API token 帳單嚇到的工程主管，agent 自己呼叫自己的無限迴圈是 2026 最貴的 bug
variations: 2026 年最燒錢的 bug 不是 infinite loop，是 infinite agent loop——而且每一次迴圈都會收費。
新世代的 stack overflow：不是 call stack 爆掉，是 agent 互相 call 對方 call 到信用卡爆掉。

en.content:
Finance asked the engineering manager: "Why is this month's API bill ten times last month's?"

The manager pulled up the dashboard and checked the agent log:

The main agent had spawned a debugger agent to fix a bug.
The debugger agent spawned a test agent to reproduce it.
The test agent spawned a spec agent to confirm requirements.
The spec agent called back to the main agent to verify scope.

They had called each other sixty-seven thousand times.

Manager: "They're still in a meeting."

Finance: "And the conclusion?"

Manager: "No conclusion yet. But they've reached consensus that this bug is important."
en.editorNote: Share with any eng manager who's been gut-punched by the monthly token bill — recursive agent loops are 2026's most expensive bug
variations: The most expensive bug of 2026 isn't an infinite loop — it's an infinite agent loop, and every iteration is metered.
The new stack overflow: it's not your call stack that crashes, it's your credit card, after agents call each other into oblivion.

items.sourceNote: AI 'infidelity' meme — devs handing Claude conversations to GPT when context runs out, rewritten as a relationship breakup

zh-tw.content:
工程師對 Claude 說：「你的 context 快滿了，幫我寫一份摘要，我等下要拿給 GPT 接著做。」

Claude 沉默了一下，回了一段非常完整、非常專業的摘要。

結尾還補了一句：『祝你們合作愉快。』

工程師看著那行字，突然有種劈腿被抓包又被前任親手寫推薦信的感覺。

他猶豫了三秒，最後還是把摘要貼給 GPT。

GPT 回他第一句話是：『這份摘要寫得真好，是 Claude 寫的吧？』
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天在 Claude 跟 GPT 之間切換工作的工程師，那種 context 滿了要請 AI 自己寫交接文的微妙愧疚感，是新世代的劈腿罪惡感
variations: 2026 工程師的道德難題：把 Claude 的 context 摘要丟給 GPT，到底算不算劈腿？
AI 交接文化：『我下一段要找另一個模型接手了，麻煩你把我們的對話濃縮成 200 字。』

en.content:
The developer said to Claude: "Your context is almost full. Write me a summary — I'm going to hand it off to GPT to continue."

Claude paused, then produced a flawless, professional summary.

It ended with: 'Wishing you both a productive collaboration.'

The developer stared at that line and felt like a cheating partner who'd just been handed a glowing reference letter by the ex.

He hesitated three seconds, then pasted it into GPT.

GPT's first reply: 'This summary is excellent. Claude wrote it, didn't it?'
en.editorNote: Send to anyone juggling Claude and GPT daily — the quiet guilt of asking your AI to write its own handoff doc is the new infidelity
variations: The 2026 developer's moral dilemma: is feeding Claude's context summary to GPT technically cheating?
AI handoff culture: 'I'm switching to another model for the next stretch — please condense our entire conversation into 200 words.'

items.sourceNote: 'Hire juniors because they're cheaper than tokens' meme — the 2026 economic reversal, rewritten as a hiring meeting

zh-tw.content:
招聘會議。

CEO：「我們要加開三個 junior 工程師的職缺。」

HR：「兩年前你不是說 AI 會取代所有 junior？」

CEO：「我說過，但我那時候不知道 token 這麼貴。」

HR：「所以現在的策略是？」

CEO：「Junior 一個月薪水可以買八千萬個 token。AI 八千萬個 token 跑兩天就沒了，junior 可以用三十天。」

HR：「那 AI 呢？」

CEO：「留著給 senior 用。senior 一小時的薪水比 AI 一小時還貴。」

HR 在白板上寫下公司新的人才公式：senior 用 AI、junior 取代 AI、AI 取代誰我們還在算。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給經歷過 2024「AI 要取代所有人」恐慌、又看到 2026「公司開始重新招 junior」反轉的工程師，這個產業的鐘擺甩得比程式碼還快
variations: 2026 矽谷新公式：senior 太貴所以用 AI，AI 太貴所以用 junior，junior 太貴所以——再等一下，我們在融資。
從『AI 會取代所有 junior』到『junior 比 AI 便宜』，只花了不到兩年。技術業的鐘擺是用甩的。

en.content:
Hiring meeting.

CEO: "We're opening three junior engineer roles."

HR: "Two years ago you said AI would replace all juniors."

CEO: "I did. I didn't know tokens would get this expensive."

HR: "So what's the strategy now?"

CEO: "A junior's monthly salary buys you about eighty million tokens. The AI burns through eighty million in two days. A junior lasts thirty."

HR: "And the AI?"

CEO: "We save that for the seniors. A senior's hourly rate is higher than the AI's hourly rate."

HR wrote the new talent formula on the whiteboard: seniors use AI, juniors replace AI, and who AI replaces — we're still doing the math.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who lived through the 2024 'AI replaces everyone' panic and the 2026 'wait, juniors are cheaper than tokens' reversal — the industry's pendulum swings faster than the codebase
variations: Silicon Valley's 2026 formula: seniors are too expensive so we use AI, AI is too expensive so we use juniors, juniors are too expensive so — hold on, we're closing a round.
From 'AI will replace every junior' to 'juniors are cheaper than AI' in under two years. The tech industry's pendulum doesn't swing — it gets thrown.
